title: Praxis Special Education: Teaching Students with Intellectual Disabilities (5322) - 200 Source Questions + 300 Guided Review
description: Contains 200 original source questions, 0 assistant-authored independent practice questions, and 300 source-derived guided-review activities. The assistant audit found 103 distinct independent content kernels. Two 100-item diagnostics, an exact-blueprint 120-item simulation, detailed feedback, native learning support, and case-study decision practice for preschool-through-grade-12 ID specialists.
contentReview: Assistant audit completed: 200 independent-practice items contain 103 distinct content kernels and 97 parallel variants under the normalized test. 300 additional activities are guided reasoning transformations, not independent exam questions. The 500-distinct-question target is not met.
simulationLabel: 120-question Teaching Students with Intellectual Disabilities simulation
officialCaseStudyNote: The official exam includes some selected-response questions based on an approximately 500-word case study.
disclaimer: Independent preparation material, not affiliated with or endorsed by ETS or AAIDD. Results are not official forms, scaled scores, pass predictions, licenses, diagnoses, eligibility, placement, adaptive-behavior determinations, medical or legal advice, or substitutes for individualized assessment and current requirements.

domains.label: Development and Characteristics of Students with Intellectual Disabilities
Planning and Managing the Learning Environment

items.prompt: When interpreting development and adaptive behavior for a student with an intellectual disability, a kindergarten teacher notices that one child reaches several language milestones later than classmates. What is the most appropriate first interpretation?
choices: Development follows broad patterns, but individual variation requires information from multiple sources and settings before conclusions are drawn.
Any difference from a class average proves a disability.
The child should immediately receive a fixed disability label.
Chronological age no longer matters when planning instruction.
items.rationale: Milestones provide useful reference points, not stand-alone diagnoses. Educators should document functioning across domains, contexts, and time and involve qualified team members before attributing a pattern to disability. For 5322 preparation, connect intellectual and adaptive evidence to individualized, age-respectful instruction; presume competence; integrate communication, functional, academic, social, self-determination, and transition goals; fade support toward agency; and monitor meaningful participation and generalization.

For 5322 preparation, connect intellectual and adaptive evidence to individualized, age-respectful instruction; presume competence; integrate communication, functional, academic, social, self-determination, and transition goals; fade support toward agency; and monitor meaningful participation and generalization.

items.prompt: When interpreting development and adaptive behavior for a student with an intellectual disability, a student solves multistep problems during small-group instruction but rarely begins the same work during a noisy whole-class period. What should the teacher examine first?
choices: Assume the student has forgotten the underlying skill.
Compare task demands, environmental conditions, supports, and performance across the two contexts.
Lower every academic expectation for the student.
Treat the difference as deliberate noncompliance.
items.rationale: Performance reflects an interaction among learner skills, task demands, supports, and context. Comparing conditions can identify a removable barrier before the team changes expectations or infers a stable trait. For 5322 preparation, connect intellectual and adaptive evidence to individualized, age-respectful instruction; presume competence; integrate communication, functional, academic, social, self-determination, and transition goals; fade support toward agency; and monitor meaningful participation and generalization.

items.prompt: When interpreting development and adaptive behavior for a student with an intellectual disability, a teacher wants to understand a learner’s self-regulation needs. Which information is most useful?
choices: A single global rating completed months ago.
The individual support profile without current observations.
Observations of triggers, strategies used, recovery, and successful conditions across routines.
The average behavior of the rest of the class.
items.rationale: Self-regulation is best understood through specific, current observations of what precedes difficulty, how the learner responds, which strategies help, and whether patterns differ by setting or task. items.prompt: When interpreting development and adaptive behavior for a student with an intellectual disability, a multilingual learner has difficulty following rapid oral directions in English but demonstrates the task after a peer models it. Which interpretation is best?
choices: The performance automatically establishes an intellectual disability.
Visual modeling should be removed to reveal the true score.
Home-language information is irrelevant to educational planning.
Language access may be affecting the demonstration of knowledge, so the team should gather culturally and linguistically appropriate evidence.
items.rationale: Difference is not disability. The team should distinguish language acquisition and access from disability by using multiple methods, appropriate language supports, and information about performance in the learner’s languages. For 5322 preparation, connect intellectual and adaptive evidence to individualized, age-respectful instruction; presume competence; integrate communication, functional, academic, social, self-determination, and transition goals; fade support toward agency; and monitor meaningful participation and generalization.

items.prompt: When interpreting development and adaptive behavior for a student with an intellectual disability, a middle school student with an intellectual disability is embarrassed when an adult uses age-inappropriate materials for a functional routine. What principle should guide revision?
choices: Use age-respectful materials while matching the instructional steps and supports to the learner’s current needs.
Match all materials to developmental level regardless of age.
Stop teaching functional skills until grade-level mastery is reached.
Ask peers to complete the routine for the student.
items.rationale: Instruction can be individualized without sacrificing dignity. Materials and contexts should be chronological-age respectful, while prompts, task steps, and response modes are adapted to the learner’s present performance. items.prompt: When interpreting development and adaptive behavior for a student with an intellectual disability, a student performs a social routine only with one familiar adult. Which plan best promotes generalization?
choices: Repeat the skill only in the original setting until it is perfect.
Practice the same skill with planned variations in people, materials, and natural settings, then fade prompts.
Introduce several unrelated target skills at the same time.
Wait for generalization to occur without programming it.
items.rationale: Generalization is more likely when instruction deliberately varies relevant people, materials, and settings, reinforces the skill in natural routines, and gradually transfers stimulus control away from adult prompts. For 5322 preparation, connect intellectual and adaptive evidence to individualized, age-respectful instruction; presume competence; integrate communication, functional, academic, social, self-determination, and transition goals; fade support toward agency; and monitor meaningful participation and generalization.

items.prompt: When interpreting development and adaptive behavior for a student with an intellectual disability, a team is writing a learner profile. Which statement is most useful for planning?
choices: The student is described only by a global support-needs label in most situations.
The student has autism and therefore prefers routines.
The student follows a two-step visual routine independently and needs one verbal prompt when the sequence changes.
The student is developmentally much younger than peers.
items.rationale: Objective descriptions identify a demonstrated strength, a specific condition, and the support needed. Global labels and assumptions based on category do not tell the team what to teach or how to measure change. For 5322 preparation, connect intellectual and adaptive evidence to individualized, age-respectful instruction; presume competence; integrate communication, functional, academic, social, self-determination, and transition goals; fade support toward agency; and monitor meaningful participation and generalization.

items.prompt: When interpreting development and adaptive behavior for a student with an intellectual disability, a learner’s participation drops after a recent family move and change in transportation. How should the school respond?
choices: Attribute the change entirely to the individual support profile.
Remove the student from general education immediately.
Ignore nonacademic factors because they are outside instruction.
Consider environmental changes alongside disability-related needs and coordinate supports without blaming the learner or family.
items.rationale: Development and learning are influenced by environmental as well as biological factors. A responsive team gathers context, protects privacy, stabilizes access, and avoids treating a situational change as proof of a new disability need. items.prompt: When interpreting development and adaptive behavior for a student with an intellectual disability, a teacher records one difficult morning and says a student has lost a previously learned skill. What evidence is needed before changing the program?
choices: Collect repeated observations and work samples under representative conditions and compare them with prior data.
Make the change because the most recent event is always most valid.
Use the disability label as the only source of evidence.
Wait until the annual review and gather no additional data.
items.rationale: A single observation may be affected by health, context, fatigue, or task features. Repeated measures and comparison with baseline information support a more reliable decision about whether performance truly changed. For 5322 preparation, connect intellectual and adaptive evidence to individualized, age-respectful instruction; presume competence; integrate communication, functional, academic, social, self-determination, and transition goals; fade support toward agency; and monitor meaningful participation and generalization.

items.prompt: A student with an intellectual disability communicates several relevant ideas but needs support organizing them into a paragraph. Which response is best?
choices: Provide identical remediation in every academic area.
Use an accessible model, jointly construct an example, provide a visual organizer and explicit sentence-to-paragraph instruction, then fade prompts as independence grows.
Infer that the student has no ideas to communicate.
Have an adult permanently write every response.
items.rationale: Instruction should presume competence and target the observed organization need. Accessible models, communication supports, explicit instruction, guided practice, and planned prompt fading support meaningful writing and agency.
choiceRationales: Not the best answer. A documented writing need does not establish the same need in every subject. Instruction should presume competence and target the observed organization need. Accessible models, communication supports, explicit instruction, guided practice, and planned prompt fading support meaningful writing and agency.

Correct. Instruction should presume competence and target the observed organization need. Accessible models, communication supports, explicit instruction, guided practice, and planned prompt fading support meaningful writing and agency.

Not the best answer. Difficulty organizing a paragraph does not mean the learner lacks ideas. Instruction should presume competence and target the observed organization need. Accessible models, communication supports, explicit instruction, guided practice, and planned prompt fading support meaningful writing and agency.

Not the best answer. Permanent adult authorship replaces rather than teaches communication and writing. Instruction should presume competence and target the observed organization need. Accessible models, communication supports, explicit instruction, guided practice, and planned prompt fading support meaningful writing and agency.

items.prompt: For a student with an intellectual disability, a student with autism covers their ears during fire-drill announcements and then misses directions. Which support is most responsive?
choices: Interpret the response as intentional defiance.
Exclude the student from every future drill.
Plan a predictable, accessible warning and communication routine while teaching the student how to use it.
Require exposure without preparation until the response stops.
items.rationale: Sensory and communication needs can affect access and behavior. A predictable warning, accessible direction, and taught coping or communication response support participation while preserving safety requirements. For 5322 preparation, connect intellectual and adaptive evidence to individualized, age-respectful instruction; presume competence; integrate communication, functional, academic, social, self-determination, and transition goals; fade support toward agency; and monitor meaningful participation and generalization.

items.prompt: For a student with an intellectual disability, a student with ADHD begins work when directions are chunked and a checklist is visible. What does this pattern suggest?
choices: The student knew the work and was choosing to fail before.
The checklist has changed the academic standard.
The disability affects intelligence rather than self-management.
Executive-function supports are improving task initiation, organization, and monitoring.
items.rationale: ADHD may affect executive functions such as initiation, working memory, inhibition, and organization. External structures can support performance without changing the learning target or implying weak ability. For 5322 preparation, connect intellectual and adaptive evidence to individualized, age-respectful instruction; presume competence; integrate communication, functional, academic, social, self-determination, and transition goals; fade support toward agency; and monitor meaningful participation and generalization.
